speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is January the 21st 1997. The survivor is Mr. Solly Irving. His last name at birth was Judensznajder. The interviewer is Ms. Simone Redbart. The interview is being conducted in London, in England, and is in English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Today's date is January the 21st, 1997. The survivor is Mr. Solly Irving. My name is Simone Redbart. And the interview is being conducted in London, England and will be in English. Could you please tell me your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The present name is Solly Irving, S-O-L-L-Y I-R-V-I-N-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was Szlama Zelman Judensznaider, J-U-D-E-N-E-N-S-Z-N-A-I-D-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell your first two names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Szlama, which is, uh, Solomon, uh, Zelman, Z-E-L-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your middle name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Middle name is Zelman, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And were you known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Officially, at the moment, is 11 of August 1930. But my original actual date since I found my birth certificate, a copy of it, was the 12th of August. I was actually born the 12th of August 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the moment, I'm presently 66, coming on 67.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ryki, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: R, uh, R-I-K-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's actually is between Warsaw and Lublin, exactly halfway between Warsaw and Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chana Necha.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, I should imagine it as C-H-A-N-A and Necha, N-E-C-H-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simple. My father was, eh, Icek in Polish and in Hebrew was Yisrael Yitzhak, Israel, eh, Isaac.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are your earliest memories of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of them? I would have to say, would be three years old when I was taken for my first haircut. You know, it's-- traditionally, in among the Hasidic sect, the boys don't get their hair cut until they're three years old. In fact, my three grandchildren had the same-- same custom, yeah. That's what I remember my first impression of my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were they like?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be honest, I'm ashamed to say, I don't remember very much. It's completely blank what they were doing-- I mean, what they were doing professionally I've learned since. But what they were like as people-- I mean, as all parents, I had fear of my father. And if anything, we stood under my mother for protection. That used to be the thing. Although he never hit us, but we always, always expected him. And the same thing my mother-- I was always told my mother was always a ill person. Yet, when the Germans came in, she was one of the strongest in the family. What her ailment were, was, uh, I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have-- did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had four sisters. I was the only boy. I had four sisters, three before me and one after me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the oldest one was Rivka. And the second one was Leah. The third one was Hinda. And the fourth one was Mendel. Mendel was the youngest. I have no recollection of their ages or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what they were like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I remember my oldest sister coming to fetch me from school 'cause school used to finish the year of-- going to school was only one year before the war broke out. And she used to collect me from school mid-day and give me some sandwiches or lunch and take me on to for the next lessons or next school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the relationship like with your other sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, very good-- being the only boy, spoiled no doubt. But I don't have, uh, very good recollections on exactly what else, what it was like.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of accommodations do you live in-- in Ryki?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last we lived in a house, a brick built house that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many bedrooms did it have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember how many bedrooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a garden?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had a backyard, yes, not so much of the garden. It was a backyard, exactly what was there I don't know. I remember that because I remember my father when the-- during the war, we dug up and hid certain valuables and also in the cellar. In the cellar, we used hid, to hide the small things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what did you used to hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Valuables, jewelry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you help in burying them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't help in burying them. Children were always been to-- told to stay out of the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever find them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. Uh, I certainly didn't and none of the family did that were survived. Because, um, I came across a cousin, with the name Chaim Judensznajder. He was the, um, the bro-- the nephew of my-- my father, one of them, anyway. And, uh, he told me he jumped from the first train, from the ghetto was liquidated. And he was trying to hide in our house. And he told me that everything was dug up within hours of being liquidated [INAUDIBLE] the Jews taking out everything [INAUDIBLE]. They knew who to go-- also we were, we were in business. My father was in grain business together with his brothers. And I think he had-- they had partners, as well. So, obviously, the people knew, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the train that your Chaim was on, where was it going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've learned since it was going to Sobibor. I always felt, for many years, I thought they would went Treblinka, which was at Lublin. But since Martin Gilson, Matthew Gilbert, called the of and he gives clear all the towns and the dates and everything. So since then, I-- I assumed that it was Sobibor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what other childhood memories do you have? You mentioned in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Good ones or bad ones?

speaker: interviewer
text: Both. What the-- if you tell me the good ones.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the-- the good ones is because, um, as a boy, we played, going to cheder, you know cheder. But the unpleasant memories is when I first went to school, and I was abused as a Jew, as a Jewish boy. And it was terrible. And originally my mother didn't want me to go to a mixed school. But my father, I suppose, being in business he wanted me to grow up also and to have a better education. Because the cheder only taught Jewish religious things, most of them anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before-- year before the war broke out, my father bribed the headmaster, and, uh, he took me to school. And the schools were always outside the center of the town, certainly by-- by church, mostly by churches. And, uh, one day I was attacked coming home from school by boys and rubbed my mouth with ham, abused me, hit me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I came home, obviously, cried for my father. Next day my father took me to the school, to the headmaster and explained what happened. And the headmaster took me around in their parade, assembly. And he said, show me who did this to you. Of course, I couldn't tell him. But he pulled me by my ear. He didn't take me by my hand. I mean, I couldn't have been that much bigger, but I was a young-- I'm small today. So I was much, much smaller then. I could have only been what, eight, nine years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the names of the boys that were in your class?

words.text: boys that were in your class?

speaker: interviewee
text: None at all. None at all. I was the only boy sitting with a beret in school, with my payot-- you know what payot are-- tucked under. So, obviously, I was a target for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you wear a uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, no. In fact, my mother insisted the school caps, in those days in Polish schools, was a-- was almost like a-- if you ever seen a Polish soldier, they have the-- like a cross on the top sticking out there. And so my mother objected. So I was the only one wearing with a beret instead of a school cap.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember any lessons that you were taught?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's completely out of my-- I was only there a year, if that, before broke-- war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other childhood memories do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we played games, uh, going with my parents in the summer months to, um, to the forest for holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of games did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the biggest game in those, it used to be like-- from a piece of wire, we used to make like a-- and for wheel, take a wheel from the bicycle, and roll it along, and catch it in that piece of wire. So it shouldn't fall over. There was an, also, um, uh, here you have cricket. We didn't play cricket. I played football, tried to play football. We also had a game, it was a piece of wood, short piece of wood with two ends sharpened down and used to hit it, how far it could-- it would travel, or coins against the, the wall, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the games have names, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: They had names. I don't remember them. One-- one particular [? pallent-- ?] [? pallent, ?] used to be called, a piece of wood with the-- with the edges, eh, sharpened down so you could hit it, and have fun-- I used to -- [INAUDIBLE] used to be called [? pallent. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who used to play that, the boys or girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boys, mostly. But we-- I didn't play much with the girls. We very much, uh, of the Orthodox sects, and I don't remember playing with girls, to be honest, even at that young age, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your neighbors around you, were they religious, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Ryki a very religious town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ryki, was yes-- as far as I know, yes, it was very Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many synagogues did it have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many, I wouldn't-- yeah, there was one large synagogue, which belonged to the town. And then each sect had their own [NON-ENGLISH], yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many sects were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a few. I don't know exactly how many but quite a few.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which sect--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because each offshoot had their own-- the rabbi's offshoot had their own. And if you belong to a [NON-ENGLISH]. If you belong to the shoe menders, they had their own synagogue. The tailors had their own synagogue. So each one had their own, their own little pla-- parish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which sect did you belong to?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a place called [NON-ENGLISH], which was [? near Pol-- ?] near Warsaw. And that's an offshoot of [? Gehr ?], or the famous [? Gehr ?] was an offshoot where one of the relations, a son, son-in-law, nephew or somebody established a sect, and that's what, uh, we went to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Um, did you wear any specific type of clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You'll see in the picture, I used to wear little black coat from the time-- from the time I was five-- black coat and black socks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who gave you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, uh, coat, I remember distinctly when I started learning chumash, my grandfather, my father's side, gave me that. And also a gartel, you know [INAUDIBLE] where a dividing, uh, belt on top of the coat. It's-- it's not called a belt. It's called a gartel, a divider between the heavenly and the bottom part of the body, spiritually.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember, like, celebrating all the festivals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, uh, in exactly what way I don't remember. But I remember when I started learning chumash that was, uh, that was something. The party, it went on for hours, and after I had to give a speech, um, which I was thankful, told what to say and how to say it, you know. And, um, it was almost like a bar mitzvah today.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember a lot about your dad still?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do on the religious side. Yes, yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the festivals, do you remember getting dressed up for Purim or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, oh yeah, of course, always Purim, and always it was customary twice a year, you got new clothes-- Pesach and Sukkos. You always got something new-- however poor we were then how poor or how rich we were, we always had new things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember you got given?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it came naturally. Well, whether-- if you needed shoes, you got shoes. If you needed a-- a pair of trousers, you got a pair of trousers, or shirt, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sisters, as well, did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They also got presents. And they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would imagine so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do they do? Did they also learn chumash or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly what the-- my oldest sister went to gymnasium, which is all year high school. Um, I seem to remember she almost finished, because she worked for my father's business parlor at the time. What else she did after, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was Rivka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your other sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, they were still in-- learning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you all--

speaker: interviewee
text: Whether they-- I don't think they went to-- to high schools at that time. Maybe they went to girl's [? sams ?] or something like that, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you learn together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I was always tested by my father on a Friday night-- excuse me-- what I've learned. Actually he paid for it, because he wanted to know what I learned. It wasn't, um-- education, like even today, a lot of it is private. It's not state aided. It certainly wasn't in Poland. I'm sure it wasn't state aided.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you always get the answers right or what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm sure I didn't. I'm sure I didn't, but I was very friendly with the, um, the son of our town rabbis. And we always learned together. And [INAUDIBLE], I went to his father, and he came to my father always to be tested. It was mostly on a Friday night or Shabbos depending on when there was a-- it was Friday night. In the summer, it was Shabbos afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the walk to from the houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember the walk going through to him, yes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your daily routine like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember exactly the detail, but I would imagine when you get up in the morning you had certain prayers. I'm sure I must have gone with my father if I was old enough to go with him to [? Shul ?], uh, like so many today. And-- and then went to school, [NON-ENGLISH] school, came home for lunch, met my sis-- my sis, met by my sister, gave me some lunch, and take me on to the next stage of my education.

speaker: interviewer
text: So didn't have much time to-- for play, but we always found [? time, ?] with boys we always found time. I'm sure we found time to play, mostly winters, skating on our hills, or sliding down some little hill, and making up, um, slates or whatever, you know. I'm sure we [INAUDIBLE] doing the same thing as boys do today, maybe more modern way today you got computers. So they sit in those and watch television. We never had television or radio. I don't remember having radio at home. And, um, with whom did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boys in the cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever mix with your school friends socially?

speaker: interviewee
text: This the year of the school, nah. They, they were mostly non-Jews anyway. And the Jewish boys I used to meet in the cheder. We used to play together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as a family, where you a very close family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would think so. I don't know how close we were. I'm sure we were. In those days, most families were close, rich or poor. Family was something that we could learn today, from today, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that you had your hair cut in 1933 when you were three years old. Uh, that was the time also when Hitler came into power. How did this affect your due--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, um, when he came into power. I wouldn't, I was too young to remember. The only time I remember when this came about was, um, when war broke out, and the planes kept coming over our town. And people are running to hide. And they used to come down so low, you could see the pilots, almost their eyeballs and shooting at the people. That's my first recollection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever know anyone that got shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't. I'm sure I must have done. I'm sure, but who exactly individually, I-- I wouldn't know. I don't remember. It's a terrible thing to, uh, to have gone through a life and not remembering many things with them in childhood. I used to be so jealous when I came across recent years of friends, say, oh, I've known them so long. I've known them so long, and they did things together. I used to be terribly envious of them. And now, when I think of it, I also know people now that I've known 50 years, people I met up in the camps [INAUDIBLE] and since the camps, yeah. So it's-- it's a long time. Yet, um, when you think about-- I was still-- I was envious. There were two very close friends of mine. Whenever we got together, used to talk about holidays that you have to get together from school and the things they used to do in school. And I always used to think, if only I had someone who remembered me from home, yeah, what I was like.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your friends' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends were here. It was one of my closest friends, Sidney Wagner. He lives up the road here and David Halo. These are some of my closest friends, from English boys. I'm talking about English bo-- boys. Of course, many from my group that I know and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And which groups' this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 458 Society, Holocaust survivors. We very close. And also the boys that I've spent the time with in the different hostels since I've came to England or met [? up in ?] Theresienstadt or met up during the war-- my longest friend, another one is still alive. It's two actually. One lives in Australia. His name is, um, Jack Feingold, Fein. He calls himself Fein, used to be [? Feingzer, ?] calls himself Fein, that I visited uh, on a year ago in Australia. He's been here several time--he actually came with me to England. And he left for Australia he had two uncles that survived the war and took him over. They went after the war to Australia. And they took him, and brought him to Melbourne. Unfortunately, when I went this time-- I remember that his uncles from the camps, early camp days-- and they weren't alive anymore. So I was very sorry. One dies within weeks before I got there. So I was rather upset. I thought they might have been able to tell me about my father, because they were much older, and they knew my father. But I was too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever met anyone else that knows your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came another one that came across, much younger [INAUDIBLE] my age, who said to me, he remembered me from home and he met my father. And I said, what he look like? He says, like all Jews with a beard and a certain type cap. You know it didn't help very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back again, when you mentioned about the Polish army coming in, what were they like?

speaker: interviewee
text: German army.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German army, sorry, coming in-- what-- what were your first impressions of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, uh, when the planes came over, and after they-- it-- it-- it died down a bit, we, um, his children-- my parents took me away to another town called [? Jelehova. ?] And-- and there we've spent a few months. And when we came back German occupation [INAUDIBLE] and it wasn't too bad. My father carried on business with him. I remember one year the army provided them with an escort and a lorry to go to Lublin, to bring back flour for Pesach. I remember that. When exactly it was I don't remember. But so we must have carried them. He must have had some-- some-- made some friends in the German occupation now, because I have a telegram which was sent via that person. Now we had no-- no way of doing it at [INAUDIBLE] unless he had, um, somebody he knew in the occupation forces that, um, would have given that to help to send a telegram to my uncle to Palestine who lived in [? Haifa then. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to say all was well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your uncle go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: He left in 1933-34.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yaacov Finkelman. He was the brother of my mother, one-- one of the brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- well, he-- he-- lived in-- in-- Palestine, in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And does he remember your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, we never spoke much about it. I tried to talk to him many times about it. He always avoided talking. He felt very guilty of-- because apparently he, uh-- he had-- obviously things were tough in Palestine in those days. And my father, I believe, asked him what he should do, whether he should to come to Palestine or not. And he advised him not to. You see, things were hard. He had to work as a laborer, in the building material on the-- the land. And, uh, he knew that my father, [PAUSES] or my mother, rather, was the oldest of-- of the family. And there was still sisters of my mother that weren't married. And she sort of looked after them. So he felt a bit guilty for advising my father, not directly, but hinted to him that it wasn't the place for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your relationship like with your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good, almost-- almost like a father to me. And in fact when I, when I was demobbed from the Israeli army in 1949 he wanted me very much to come and live with him. He had two daughters and, uh, obviously his wife And his wife, particularly-- she was like a mother to me. They wanted me very much to come live with him and for me to go to college, get an education, which I never had. But, um, I felt it was wrong of me to stay on, because things were very, very tough for them even. He was in the army. He was trying to bring up two daughters himself. And, uh, things were not easy, and I felt that I should give them a little bit rest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Minucha.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sa-- one-- the oldest one is Sarah and the second one is Shoshana. And we still very close. Although they marry and aunt and uncle died. And-- But I'm close to my cousins. And we're very much in touch with each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you going back then to when the German planes came in, how, um, ho-- what happened after the planes came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, exactly what happened-- I mean, I'm sure after awhile they stopped coming over to find, and they occupied Poland. We came back into town, and things carried on as normal. And then, um, sudden they created a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto must have been-- must have been-- ghetto must have been I would reckon '40, '41 they establish a ghetto. And, uh, and they designate a certain area. We were lucky that our house was in the ghetto, so we didn't have to move out. But a lot of people came in. And there's one, uh, one man in Israel, at the moment, called Moishe Oppenheim that lived-- he spent, his family, spent time in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he the only family that spent time in your house or were there others?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm sure their others, but I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, until-- till the war, it was only just our own family, but then, obviously, my mother's sisters and brother came in and this family, Oppenheim, came in. And there must have been others, as well. It was quite a lot.

words.text: well. It was quite a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember-- do you remember sharing the house with the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions like in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very good. Not very good. I don't know how long the ghetto was established. But, um-- but then suddenly town was surrounded one morning. It was a commotion and town was surrounded. And then German lorries came in, surrounded the center of the town, and took everybody into-- into the mid-- square of the town and started transporting them to-- away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where they were being transported to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't, but my father, somehow, managed to bribe  a guard to let me through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the guards' name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember the guards' name now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your father telling you that he expected to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father-- I remember my father coming to-- to the house, and pulling me by my hand. And he said, I want you to escape, and to [INAUDIBLE] I think there should be somebody of the family left. And I will tell you where. Make for such and such farm, and that I've given the men certain valuables and he will look after you. So he thought, obviously. And so I escaped. [PAUSES] Don't ask me how I managed to escape. I don't really know how. There was, um, Germans going up and down the road. And I was dressed-- I must have been dressed in the almost in the uniform that I wore with the black coat. And so I didn't look Polish. Why they didn't stop me, I don't know. And then with minutes escaping a sister, my second older sister, Leah, came running and we managed-- met up with me. And she managed to-- to escape with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your other, the rest of your family, your other sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I know is that the man, the farmer that I made for, my father told me to-- to go to eventually came home that night and he told me, he so. He took a horse and cart. They were ordered to bring horse and carts to town to transport the Jewish people. Those who could walk had to walk, and those who couldn't, had the older or the infirm put on the tracks and the horse and cart to be transported to the station.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape one.
